title: Pricing & Monetisation Maturity

introduction.subtitle: Commercial maturity assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Pricing & Monetisation Maturity helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Value Understanding, Packaging & Segmentation, Price Architecture & Governance, Experimentation & Realisation. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How is willingness to pay understood?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Value Understanding; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Value Understanding, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Value Understanding has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Value Understanding is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Value Understanding: How is willingness to pay understood
conceptHelp.summary: Value Understanding means knowing what customers value, how much that value is worth, and where willingness to pay comes from. This question narrows the concept to "How is willingness to pay understood?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Value Understanding matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show the current model behind "How is willingness to pay understood?", the assumptions behind it, the owner, and the decision that changes when the numbers move.

questions.text: How well can the team quantify customer value?

options.text: A. The answer is broad, assumed, or based mostly on internal belief.
B. There is a high-level description, but segmentation, trigger, or buyer evidence is thin.
C. The answer is defined for a specific segment with documented behaviour, alternatives, and decision criteria.
D. The answer is validated across repeated cases and directly informs targeting, messaging, prioritisation, and trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How often is pricing tested or reviewed?

options.text: A. It happens rarely, reactively, or only when a crisis forces attention.
B. It happens occasionally, but timing depends on individual initiative or urgency.
C. There is a defined cadence with clear participants, owners, and triggers.
D. The cadence is frequent enough to change decisions, includes quality checks, and improves from outcomes.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Experimentation & Realisation: How often is pricing tested or reviewed
conceptHelp.summary: Experimentation & Realisation means testing price and packaging while measuring whether expected value is actually captured in revenue and margin. This question narrows the concept to "How often is pricing tested or reviewed?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

questions.text: How clearly can you connect price to the customer's economic outcome?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows price connected to customer economics; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of price connected to customer economics, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show price connected to customer economics in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Price connected to customer economics is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Value Understanding: How clearly can you connect price to the customer's economic outcome
conceptHelp.summary: Value Understanding is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How clearly can you connect price to the customer's economic outcome?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Value Understanding matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
